The primary aim is to obtain the trace_id attached to the @api task which has made the request. To do so we need to find the future waiting for the current task to finish, which is done by recursively tracing back (recursive because @api may call a async function which makes call to another function and so on till the final call to request is made).

The numerous try/except are present because the callback function may be a normal function, a partial (hence args[0]) or a method of a task (hence .__self__).

because we have weak references, this means the element is eligible for garbage collection at any time after the `Span` is finished. It's not garbage collected if we still have references of the `Span`. This is needed because it's possible the signal mechanism is not called properly from Celery (unexpected behaviors / unhandled executions) and so even if a Span is not finished it can be garbage collected if the Tracer releases the reference to the `Span`.

We may need to check if we have to connect to other signals to be sure Spans are always finished.

3) wait 501ms, the thread from 1) kicks in and closes channel from 1)
4) someone calls `getAvailableConnection`, the queue is not empty, `poll` grabs the channel from 1) which is already closed and decides to call `getConnection` to get a new connection even though there's a good, open connection from 2)

The point I was making that if you do
```
foo = galsim.ChromaticAtmosphere(...)
foo.drawImage(...)
```
then your image doesn't correspond to anything with an astrophysical SED, it corresponds to something with a totally flat SED.  (Likewise for if you did this with ```ChromaticOpticalPSF```.)   In other words, let's say you have an object with some SED, that you convolve with the ChromaticAtmosphere.  Its effective PSF image cannot be obtained with the above code.

You think this is not worth mentioning for ChromaticAtmosphere / ChromaticOpticalPSF?  While ideally users would have understood this, I think it doesn't hurt to mention.

A scale is just a special kind of WCS.  The simplest one, in fact.  Behind the scenes, if you set `im.scale=0.2` say for an image, this is really equivalent to `im.wcs = galsim.PixelScale(0.2)`.

However, if an image actually has a non-PixelScale wcs then trying to access it with `im.scale` will raise an exception.  So in our routines, we always need to check for wcs, not scale.

This is redundant. The files in the collection are already guaranteed to match the filespec: the filespec is just metadata about how the fileset was constructed.

The point of the `SpecAddressMapper` is to _not_ expand the fileset, and to just use the `filespec` instead. This was based on the assumption that all `FilesetWithSpec` implementations were lazy.

What I was suggesting was an abstract method on `FilesetWithSpec` something like:
```
def matches(self, paths):
    """Returns the from the given set that match this FilesetWithSpec."""
```
...and the implementation for `Eager` would be to just look in the fileset, while the implementation for `Lazy` would be to compile the regex from the filespec and use that.

Yes, the lock file is a local construct. The lock file will get updated after `yarn add eslint-plugin-react==1.2.3`. The typical usage is to make some changes to a package and have yarn automatically generate a lock file as a byproduct of the change. Check in lock file into repository and let other consumers use the lock file to reproduce the same installation. In short, `yarn add` is not guaranteed to generate the same lock file even when specifying a version number or even when pinned to a specific resolved path.

Not quite - it generates a function that can be called at the time that the Promise is resolved. If the store state indicates that the page name is still the same as when the generator was created, then it resolves as true, and the conditionalPromise resolves properly. If the page name is now different, it does not do any of the subsequent calls.

This is not about whether we should refetch data, but rather to prevent us from putting data into the store that does not match the current page (this could happen if, for example, we start loading some data, then navigate to a different route, and the promise then resolves, messing up our pageState).

Looking through this block, I don't think anything in the rest of the code in the PR actually really depends on it. The role it played for me here was in exploring how best to decouple Kolibri and Morango (i.e. what pieces belonged on which side of the fence), as well as to think through some of the potential interactions between syncing and things like MPTT (with possible `tree_id` collisions).

It sounds like it would be cleanest if I removed this entire MORANGO block for now, and moved it to the notes/specs for Morango, so that it's clear that all of this also works without Morango (other than syncing, of course). The FacilityDataset stuff would remain, but that's not *part* of Morango -- it serves three purposes:
- Scoping data for use by permissions. For example, until a user has a learner role for a class/group, the FacilityDataset the main way we would determine who has permission to edit that user.
- Storing metadata about the facility -- since Collections only have a few key fields (like name, kind, and then the MPTT stuff), we need somewhere to store additional metadata.
- Filtering/partitioning for Morango, to make it easy/efficient to query for all model instances that are part of a specific set of "facility data". So, Morango will be told to *use* this field, but they're not tightly coupled.

while True:

...
```

So the code below seems to be doing breadth-first expansion over the entire logdir directory tree in search of profile directories, which could be extremely slow/expensive when the logdir is large and on a remote filesystem.  This has come up before in real world cases, e.g. see the description of PR #1087 (the code here is basically is doing the equivalent of tf.gfile.Walk() except by hand).

This is a concern because the code is part of index_impl() which is invoked by is_active(), and is_active() runs for every plugin - even those that aren't active - every time a user reloads their TensorBoard, so we've been pushing for them to be lightweight (see #625).

I think the best way to avoid the issue would be what this TODO suggests - using EventMultiplexer, which would mean having the profile data generation side emit an events file with a summary that serves to identify that subdirectory as a "run" with profile data (the summary doesn't need to actually contain the profile data, though doing so would be a bit more future-proof).  That way the shared EventMultiplexer logic can centralize the job of searching the directory tree for data, and the profile plugin's is_active() can just do a quick and efficient call to the EventMultiplexer to see if any profile data has been discovered yet.

Another approach that would at least alleviate the time penalty is to just push the logic here into a separate thread that runs asynchronously from is_active() as discussed on #625, but that isn't ideal because then users are still paying the cost of doing this potentially expensive traversal of the full directory hierarchy on every reload (redundantly from the main EventMultiplexer traversal) - all it fixes is the latency issue but not other possible issues like sending a lot of extra GCS API calls if the logdir is on GCS, etc.

I'm happy to discuss these options in more detail if that's helpful.

A few quick comments! I think that we would want this to be either a single string, or a list of binds. For example. as a user I should be able to specify any of the following:

```
[ '/host:/container', '/both' ]
'/both'
'/host:container'
None
```
And so it would make sense to have a default of None, and then to check if the argument is not a list (and make it a list if it isn't) and then for each entry in the list, prepare the string in the format `--bind %s` %value. And I would suggest to have a function to take any of these possible inputs and return a nice bind string. For the examples above we would get:

```
"--bind /host:/container --bind /both"
"--bind /both"
"--bind /host:container"
(function wouldn't be called for this case)
```
Does that make sense? And then we can have a test for each of these cases to ensure it returns the correct! If you want to give a shot at the above go for it, if not I'm happy to do it (I pulled your branch and could code this up first thing tomorrow).
